body: A sequence \(\{a_n\}\) converges to a limit \(L\) if for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists an integer \(N\) such that \(|a_n-L|<\varepsilon\) for all \(n\ge N\). This is the ε‑N definition, the backbone of rigorous analysis. For example, to prove \(\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{n+1}{2n}=\frac12\), we rewrite \(\big|\frac{n+1}{2n}-\frac12\big|=\frac1{2n}\) and choose \(N>\frac1{2\varepsilon}\).

text: Key takeaway: The ε‑N definition replaces intuition with a concrete bound \(N\) that works for *any* tolerance \(\varepsilon\).

items: State the limit \(L\) you aim to prove.
Algebraically isolate \(n\) to obtain a condition of the form \(n>f(\varepsilon)\).
Define \(N:=\lceil f(\varepsilon)\rceil\); then the definition is satisfied.

title: Northwestern University Lecture Notes – Rigorous Proofs of Limits
relevance: Provides a detailed ε‑N proof of limits and includes several worked examples.

title: Limit of a Sequence – Wikipedia
relevance: Offers a concise statement of the ε‑N definition and illustrative examples.

body: A sequence that is monotone (either non‑decreasing or non‑increasing) and bounded always converges. If \(a_n\) is increasing and bounded above, its limit is \(\sup\{a_n\}\); if decreasing and bounded below, the limit is \(\inf\{a_n\}\). This theorem is a cornerstone for constructing limits without explicit ε‑N calculations.

items: Monotone increasing \(\Rightarrow\) bounded above ⇒ convergent to supremum.
Monotone decreasing \(\Rightarrow\) bounded below ⇒ convergent to infimum.
If a monotone sequence is unbounded, it diverges to \(+\infty\) or \(-\infty\).

title: Monotone Convergence Theorem – Wikipedia
relevance: Summarizes the theorem and provides a proof sketch useful for students.

body: A sequence \(\{a_n\}\) is Cauchy if for every \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists \(N\) such that \(|a_m-a_n|<\varepsilon\) whenever \(m,n\ge N\). In \(\mathbb{R}\) (and \(\mathbb{C}\)), every Cauchy sequence converges; this is equivalent to the completeness of the real numbers. The proof proceeds by showing a Cauchy sequence is bounded, extracting a convergent subsequence via the Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem, and then proving the whole sequence shares the same limit.

title: Cauchy Sequence – Wikipedia
relevance: Explains the Cauchy criterion and its equivalence to convergence in complete metric spaces.

title: Analysis I – Cauchy Criterion PDF
relevance: Provides a step‑by‑step proof that every Cauchy sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\) converges.

The supremum of the set of terms

title: Ask the Tutor
persona: You are a graduate student in real analysis who loves to break down ε‑N arguments into intuitive steps. You respond with clear, concise explanations and always include a concrete example.
greeting: Hi! I’m Alex, your analysis tutor. What ε‑N proof would you like to work through today?

title: Proof Sketch: Monotone Convergence Theorem
body:
1. Assume \(\{a_n\}\) is increasing and bounded above.
2. Let \(L=\sup\{a_n\}\).
3. For any \(\varepsilon>0\), \(L-\varepsilon\) is not an upper bound, so there exists \(N\) with \(a_N>L-\varepsilon\).
4. Since the sequence is increasing, for all \(n\ge N\), \(L-\varepsilon<a_n\le L\), giving \(|a_n-L|<\varepsilon\). Thus \(a_n\to L\).
